The problem is to evaluate the limit:

The key takeaway is: **If $$x \to 6^{+}$$, then $$x > 6$$.**

**Step 2: Analyze the absolute value function**

The absolute value function, $$|u|$$, is a piecewise function defined as:
$$ |u| = \begin{cases} u & \text{if } u \ge 0 \\ -u & \text{if } u < 0 \end{cases} $$
In our problem, the expression inside the absolute value is $$x - 6$$. So we can define $$|x - 6|$$ as:
$$ |x - 6| = \begin{cases} x - 6 & \text{if } x - 6 \ge 0 \quad (\text{which means } x \ge 6) \\ -(x - 6) & \text{if } x - 6 < 0 \quad (\text{which means } x < 6) \end{cases} $$
**Step 3: Apply the limit condition to the absolute value**

Since we are evaluating the limit as $$x \to 6^{+}$$, we know from Step 1 that $$x > 6$$.

Looking at our piecewise definition in Step 2, the condition $$x > 6$$ falls into the first case ($$x \ge 6$$). Therefore, for this specific limit, we can replace $$|x - 6|$$ with just $$(x - 6)$$.

**Step 4: Substitute and simplify the expression**

Now we can rewrite the original limit by substituting $$x - 6$$ for $$|x - 6|$$:
$$ \underset{ x \to 6^{+} }{ \lim } \frac{6 - x}{x - 6} $$

$$ \underset{ x \to 6^{+} }{ \lim } -1 $$

The limit of a constant is the constant itself.
$$ \underset{ x \to 6^{+} }{ \lim } -1 = -1 $$

### Final Answer:

The value of the limit is **-1**.
